q: With respect to drug-enzyme interaction, identify the wrong statement:
question: With respect to drug-enzyme interaction, identify the wrong statement:
options: Non-Competitive inhibitor binds to the allosteric site
Allosteric inhibitor changes the enzyme's active site
Allosteric inhibitor competes with the enzyme's active site
Competitive inhibitor binds to the enzyme's active site
solution:
Some drugs do not bind to the Enzyme's active site. These bind to a different site of enzyme which called allosteric site.

This binding of inhibitor at allosteric site changes the shape of the active site in such a way that substrate can not recognize it. Such inhibitor is known as Non-competitive inhibitor.

q: The correct order of conductivity of ions in water is:
question: The correct order of conductivity of ions in water is:
solution:
→Li+Na+K+Rb+Cs+Hydration energy↑

As the size of gaseous ion decreases, it gets more hydrated in water and hence, the size of aqueous ion increases. When this bulky ion move in solution, it experience greater resistance and hence lower conductivity.

Size of gaseous ion: Cs+>Rb+>K+>Na+

Size of aqueous ion: Cs+<Rb+<K+<Na+

Conductivity: Cs+>Rb+>K+>Na+

solution:
From the above diagram, it is clear that z, iz are perpendicular to each other.

Here, base =z, height =iz

So, area of the triangle formed by the vertices i.e., 

A=12×base×height

⇒A=12×z×iz

⇒A=12z2

Hence, the required area is 12z2.

⇒limx→02sinsinx+x2sinx-sinx2sinx+x2x-sinx2x4×sinx+x2x-sinx2=1k

Apply L'Hospital rule for an indeterminate form of 00, we get, 

⇒2×(1+1)2×12×16=1 k

solution:
kx+y+z=1

x+ky+z=k

x+y+zk=k2

Δ=K111K111K=KK2-1-1K-1+11-K

=K3-K-K+1+1-K

=K3-3 K+2

=(K-1)2 K+2

For K=1

Δ=Δ1=Δ2=Δ3=0

But for K=-2, at least one out of Δ1,Δ2,Δ3 are not zero

Hence for no solution, K=-2

question: The sum of possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>31</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Given tan-1x+1+cot-11x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-1x+1+tan-1x-1=tan-1831,   ∴tan-1x=cot-11x, x>0

⇒tan-1x+1+x-11-x+1x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-12x1-x+1x-1=tan-1831

Taking tangent both the sides we get, 

(x+1)+(x-1)1-x2-1=831

⇒2x2-x2=831

⇒4x2+31x-8=0

⇒x=-8,14

But, if x=14

tan-1x+1∈0,π2

and cot-11x-1∈π2,π

⇒LHS>π2 and RHS<π2 (Not possible)

Hence, x=-8=-324.

⇒A10-Adj2A10=1024102301-1024-104755201048576=010485750-1048575

⇒detA10-Adj2A10=010485750-1048575=0

⇒E=detA4+0

⇒E=-2-04

⇒E=16

solution:
Energy of H -atom is E=-13.6Z2/n2 for H-atom Z=1 and for ground state, n=1

⇒E=-13.6×1212=-13.6 eV

Now for carbon atom (single ionised), Z=6

E=-13.6Z2n2=-13.6 (given)

⇒n2=62⇒n=6

q:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
question:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
solution:
Positive zero error =0.2 mm

Main scale reading =8.5 cm

Vernier scale reading=6×0.01=0.06 cm

Final reading=8.5+0.06-0.02=8.54 cm

q: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Since each vibrational mode has 2 degrees of freedom hence total vibrational degrees of freedom =48

f=3+3+48=54

γ=1+2f=2827=1.03

q: Two ideal polyatomic gases at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are mixed so that there is no loss of energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the degrees of freedom, masses, number of molecules of the first and second gas respectively, the temperature of mixture of these two gases is:
question: Two ideal polyatomic gases at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are mixed so that there is no loss of energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the degrees of freedom, masses, number of molecules of the first and second gas respectively, the temperature of mixture of these two gases is:
solution:
Let the final temperature of the mixture be T.

Since, there is no loss in energy.

ΔU=0

⇒F12n1RΔT+F22n2RΔT=0

⇒F12n1RT1-T+F22n2RT2-T=0

⇒T=F1n1RT1+F2n2RT2 F1n1R+F2n2R=F1n1 T1+F2n2 T2 F1n1+F2n2
